coredata.dc:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide and dendrimer and application in photothermal therapy: An experimental and theoretical study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Sheini, Azarmidokht
Taherpour, Avat(Arman)
Maghsudi, Maryam
Farajmand-Amirabadi, Sakineh
Kouchak, Maryam
Rahbar, Nadereh
Sabaeian, Mohammad
Alidadi, Hadis
coredata.dc:description: The present study was conducted to synthesize nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) through amidative/oxidative cutting of graphene oxide using H2O2 and G0 PAMAM dendrimer as oxidizing and amidative agents, respectively. G0 PAMAM dendrimer, as a multiamine, was utilized for doping N into GQDs and passivation of the active surface of GQDs to obtain N-doped GQDs and amine-modified GQDs. The N-GQDs exhibited a broad absorption band from the visible to the first near infra-red (NIR) region with a photoluminescence. Interestingly, the prepared the N-GQDs exhibited reasonable photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 53.45% upon 808 nm laser irradiation). The structures of N-GQDs were investigated by a series characterization method. Moreover, a theoretical study was carried out employing quantum mechanical (QM) method on the structures of the designed graphene oxide and N-GQDs profiles to obtain the energy gap. Subsequently, the absorption and photoluminescence mechanisms of the N-GQDs were proposed. Following the evaluation of biocompatibility, the N-GQDs were evaluated to their photothermal application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Graphene oxide
G0 PAMAM
Photothermal efficiency
Quantum mechanical method

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of G0 PAMAM dendrimer
Photoluminescence and photothermal properties of N-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Nanotechnology Research Center and Research Council of Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank for the financial support of this research work (grant No. N-9811 ) provided by the Nanotechnology Research Center and Research Council of Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs, B) Full survey XPS spectra and C) C 1s, N1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs.
A) Raman spectra and B) XRD pattern of the N-GQDs,.
A) TEM image at scale bar of 50 nm, B) HR-TEM image and C) TEM image at bar scale of 200 nm.

A) AFM image of the N-GQDs, B) AFM phase imaging of N-GQDs, white circles show bright dots with crystalline structure and yellow circles show dots with amorphous structures C) Height profile of N-GQDs along the line from a to b.
A) UV–vis absorption of diluted solution of N-GQDs, dendrimer, and the graphene oxide, B) UV–vis absorption of concentrated solution of N-GQDs.
Experimentally derived optical band gap curve from the Tauc plot equation for (A) N-GQDs, (B) Graphene oxide.

A) PL spectra of the diluted solution of N-GQDs excited at different wavelengths and the inset shows PL spectra of the concentrated solution of N-GQDs excited at different wavelengths. B) Photographs of left side shows the concentrated sample of N-GQDs and right side shows the diluted sample of N-GQDs under UV light, C) UV–vis absorption (black), PL excitation (blue), and emission (red) spectra of N-GQDs.
Cell viability of different concentrations of N-GQDs on three cell lines, including A) MDA-MB-231, B) MCF-7 and C) SK-BR-3 for 24 h.

Investigation of photothermal property of the N-GQDs. A) Temperature changes of the deionized water (as control) and different concentrations of N-GQDs solutions were irradiated with the laser in 10 min (808 nm, 1.29 W/cm2). B) Curve of temperature changes of different concentrations of N-GQDs under 10 min laser irradiation. C) Cooling curve of CDs of N-GQDs (250 μg/mL) versus time; laser was turned off 600 s after initiation of irradiation. D) Data obtained from cooling time versus -lnθ acquired from the cooling curve of Fig. 10C).
The optimized modeled structure of A) graphene oxide, B) PAMAM-G0, and C) the N-GQDs based on the experimental conditions.
The calculated maximum wavelength (in nm) of the optimized modeled structure of A) graphene oxide and B) N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide and dendrimer and application in photothermal therapy: An experimental and theoretical study

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, School of Pharmacy, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study was conducted to synthesize nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) through amidative/oxidative cutting of graphene oxide using H2O2 and G0 PAMAM dendrimer as oxidizing and amidative agents, respectively. G0 PAMAM dendrimer, as a multiamine, was utilized for doping N into GQDs and passivation of the active surface of GQDs to obtain N-doped GQDs and amine-modified GQDs. The N-GQDs exhibited a broad absorption band from the visible to the first near infra-red (NIR) region with a photoluminescence. Interestingly, the prepared the N-GQDs exhibited reasonable photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 53.45% upon 808 nm laser irradiation). The structures of N-GQDs were investigated by a series characterization method. Moreover, a theoretical study was carried out employing quantum mechanical (QM) method on the structures of the designed graphene oxide and N-GQDs profiles to obtain the energy gap. Subsequently, the absorption and photoluminescence mechanisms of the N-GQDs were proposed. Following the evaluation of biocompatibility, the N-GQDs were evaluated to their photothermal application.

ce:section.ce:para:
The structure of graphene consists of two-dimensional (2D) crystals from sp
2 -hybridized carbon atoms in a hexagonal orderly lattice, which has attracted considerable interests due to its excellent electronic properties, such as superior thermal and electrical conductivity, high charge mobility, and biocompatibility [1,2]. In addition, recent researches have demonstrated the graphene's photoluminescent properties, which resulted in expand its application in biological and optical-related fields [3–6]. From theoretical and experimental perspectives, it has been proved that zero-bandgap and photoluminescence (PL) properties could be eﬀectively determined through the morphology of graphene-based structures (their shape, size, and thickness), doping them with dopants, and attaching them to specific functional groups [6–8]. Reducing dimensions of graphene sheets from 2D into 0D is a promising strategy to tune the band gap, which leads to formation of 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [9–11]. These structures smaller than 100 nm have quantum conﬁnement and edge eﬀects, which make them great candidates for use in certain fields, such as bioimaging, [12–16] photovoltaics [17–20], light emitting diodes [21,22], as contrast enhancement agents for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [23] and tumor therapy [24,25]. Regarding the use of GQDs in biomedicine, numerous studies have been conducted on the development of GQDs as photothermal therapy (PTT) agents. They have exhibited unusual absorption properties in NIR (650–950 nm), which are applicable in photothermal treatment of cancer [22]. In this biological transparency window, high radiation penetration through tissue is achieved due to low background fluorescence signal of biological specimens. Several researchers have reported nice works for preparing carbon-based nanomaterials with strong NIR absorptions. Zhang and Wang et al. achieved S,Se-CDs with absorption centered at ~ 526 nm and a tail to NIR range, with a PTT efficiency of 58.2% utilizing a laser of 635 nm at 2 W/cm2 [26]. Sunʼs et al. prepared citric acid-originated CDs and used formamide for n-doping into the structure. The CDs showed an absorption peak centered at 550 nm extending up to NIR range, with PTT efficiency of 43.9% induced with a laser of 671 nm at 2.5 W/cm2 [27]. Permatasariʼs et al. proposed the CDs from urea and citric acid with an absorption at 650 nm and a PTT efficiency of 54.3% employing a laser of 808-nm at 2 W/cm2 [28].

In this study, the N-GQDs with broad absorption band up to NIR were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method. The N-GQDs were obtained through the exfoliation of graphene oxide in the presence of G0 PAMAM and hydrogen peroxide. The N-GQDs showed PTT efficiency of 53.45% upon laser irradiation of 808 nm at a power intensity of 1.29 W/cm2, which is similar to the results of previous studies.

Ethylenediamine, methylacrylate, methanol, graphite powder, sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, sodium nitrate, hydrochloric acid, hydrogen peroxide solution, and MTT powder were procured from Merck. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), Penstrep, Trypsin % 0.25, L-glutamine were obtained from Bio idea and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Cegrogen.

The morphologies of the N-GQDs were investigated using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Zeiss EM10C.), operated at 100 kV accelerating voltage. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to characterize the N-GQDs at an TEC9G20 (FEL, USA) operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out using a BesTec X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Germany) with AlKα X-ray as source (hυ = 1486.6 eV). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was determined using a powder X-ray diffractometer (X′Pert Pro, Philips, Netherlands) using a Cu Kα radiation at 2-theta angles of 5–80°. Zeta potential measurement was performed using a SZ-100 Instrument (HORIBA, Japan). The FTIR spectra were obtained via a Bruker Tensor Spectrometer (Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-1650PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu., Japan) The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs were measured utilizing a Termo SCIENTIFIC – LUMINA fluorescence spectrometer. The Raman spectrum was carried out on a TakRam N1-541 spectrometer (Teksan, Iran). The AFM image was acquired on a Nano Wizard II AFM (JPK, Germany).

N-GQDs were synthesized following a multistep procedure. Primarily, graphene oxide was synthesized using modified Hummers method, according to the literature. Afterwards, G0 PAMAM was synthesized applying divergent technique. Finally, both were used for the synthesis of N-GQDs.

The modified Hummers method was used for preparation of the graphene oxide [29]. 1 g of graphite was mixed in 50 mL of sulfuric acid (98%) and 1 g of NaNO3 in a 500 mL flask and the suspension was sonicated at room temperature for 2 h. The suspension was located in an ice water bath and 6 g of KMnO4 was added to the suspension very slowly in small portions to keep the suspension temperature below 20 °C. The suspension was then heated to 80 °C and stirred for another 2 h. Next, the suspension was diluted with very slow addition of 100 mL of water, which produced a large exotherm to 98 °C. After 10 min, the suspension was diluted with increasing the water to 200 mL. Subsequently, 6 mL of 30% H2O2 was added, which changed the suspension color to orange and showed the graphene oxide formation. The final product was centrifuged and washed with 5% HCl solution for removal of the residual metal ions. Afterwards, a lot of deionized water were added for the acid removal. Ultimately, the solution was centrifuged, dried at 60 °C, and kept for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of G0 PAMAM dendrimer
ce:section.ce:para: G0 PAMAM dendrimer was synthesized according to literature [30]. Zero generation of PAMAM dendrimer (G0 PAMAM) with ethylenediamine core were synthesized in a stepwise process. The synthesis involved the stepwise addition of two monomers, methyl acrylate, and ethylenediamine to tetrafunctional ethylenediamine core. For the synthesis of G-0.5 PAMAM dendrimer a methanolic solution of ethylenediamine (20 mmol, 0.6 mL) was added dropwise to a methanolic solution of methyl acrylate (100 mmol, 9.062 mL) upon argon atmosphere. The solution was stirred for 30 min at 0°C and then stirred for another 24 h at room temperature. For solvent removal, we used vacuum evaporator apparatus under reduced pressure at 40 °C. For the synthesis of G0 PAMAM dendrimer, a solution of G-0.5 (10 mmol, 4 g) was added dropwise to a methanolic solution of ethylenediamine (120 mmol, 8 mL) in methanol at 0 °C under argon atmosphere. The stirring was then followed for 96 h at room temperature. For removal of ethylenediamine, it was intensively dried in a vacuum desiccator over concentrated sulfuric acid.

To synthesize the N-GQDs, 0.1 g graphene oxide powder was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and sonicated (60 min) to form a homogeneous mixture. The mixture was subjected to stirring and heating at 160 °C and 10 mL of H2O2 was rapidly added. Afterwards, 1 g of G0 PAMAM was slowly and carefully added to the mixture (as soon as the increase in dendrimer produced a lot of bubbles and heat in the reaction vessel). The precursors mixture was placed under reflux conditions at 160 °C and 4 h. N-GQDs were obtained with black color while nanosized graphene oxide particles were also present in the final solution. Larger solid particles were filtered by the syringe filter (0.22 µm). The final product was then dialyzed with a dialysis bag (Molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) = 1000 Da) for 24 h until the residual materials were removed.

The photothermal properties of the sample were determined as: 300 μL of aqueous solutions of the N-GQDs with different concentrations from 0 to 1000 μg mL−1 were irradiated by an 808-nm laser at 1.2 W/cm2 for 600 s. Deionized water was applied as a negative control. The laser was inserted in a perpendicular position to the solutions and each of the samples were irradiated for 600 s. A thermocouple probe was entered into the solution perpendicularly to the laser light path. The solution temperature was registered every 60 s using a digital thermometer. Subsequently, photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the N-GQDs was determined through the following equation [31]:
(1)
η
=
hA
(
T
Max
−
T
Surr
)
−
Q
Diss
I
( 1 −
10
A
λ
)
where A shows the surface area of the sample container, h shows the heat-transfer coefficient, and TMax and TSurr denote the temperatures of the sample and the room, respectively. I represent the laser power, QDiss denotes the heat caused by light absorption of the surroundings (the solution and water), and Aλ is the sample absorbance at a wavelength of 808 nm (the laser wavelength).

The MCF-7, SK-BR-3, and MDA-MB-231 cell lines were used to assess the biocompatibility of the N-GQDs. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a concentration of 1 × 10 4 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following 24 h of incubation at 37 °C, the culture medium was discarded and the wells were treated with different concentrations (0–400 μg mL−1) of the N-GQDs for 24 h. Three-replicate wells were prepared per concentration. Subsequently, 20 μL of MTT (5.0 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added into each well and incubated at CO2 atmosphere for 4 h at 37 °C. The culture medium was then replaced with 100 μL of DMSO for each well and the absorbance of the wells was determined at 570 nm.

The N-GQDs were facilely prepared through solvothermal treatment of graphene oxide in the presence of G0 PAMAM and H2O2 followed by a dialysis process. Herein, G0PAMAM was applied as the dopant and an agent for H2O2 dissociation and also for passivation of the active surface of the N-GQDs. The synergistic effect of G0PAMAM and H2O2 had an important role in the successful synthesis of N-GQDs. The N-GQDs manifested a strong and broad absorption band from UV–vis to NIR, which could be used in some fields, such as photothermal therapy and photocatalysts. Graphene oxide was initially prepared through the modified Hummer’s method. Afterwards, G0PAMAM was synthesized via divergent method using ethylenediamine as core and methyl acrylate as monomer. The graphene oxide reacted with H2O2 and G0PAMAM at 160 °C for 4 h. Finally, the yellow-emitting N-GQDs were obtained with a broad absorption band in NIR. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman measurements and Zeta potential analysis were employed for investigation of the surface groups, states and the chemical composition of N-GQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were applied for morphological investigations. The optical properties were evaluated with PL emission, excitation spectra and UV–vis spectrum.

Fig. 1A exhibits the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs. This spectrum comprises various stretching vibrations corresponding to the functional groups of graphene oxide and G0 PAMAM. In the FTIR spectra, the band at 1715 cm−1 is attributed to CO bond of carboxyl or carbonyl. A broadened and strong band observed at 1595 cm−1 referred to the CC and CN bonds [32,33]. The stretching vibrations at 3170–3418 cm−1 are attributed to N − H, -NH2, and O−H bonds, respectively [34]. The bands at 2930 and 2851 cm−1 are ascribed to C−H bonds which are stronger in comparison to the spectrum of graphene oxide and point out the presence of the carbon chain of G0 PAMAM entered to the N-GQDs. 1263, 1402, and 1080 cm−1 peaks are associated with C-O stretching, the C–N bending, and C–O–C stretching, respectively [35,36]. Thus, FTIR provides evidence of the presence of types of nitrogen and oxygen functionality in the N-GQDs structure.

FTIR data was further verified through XPS analysis. The survey XPS spectra, presented in Fig. 1B, revealed the characteristic peaks of C1s, O1s, and N1s respectively at 285, 400, and 531 eV, indicating that the N-GQDs are consist of C, O, and N elements without any impurities. The high-resolution C1s spectrum displayed four binding energies at 285, 286.7, 287.7, and 288.8 eV, which are assignable to sp2 carbons (CC), sp3 carbons (C-O/C-N), (CO/CN), and (OC–O/OC–N2) respectively (Fig. 1C) [27,29]. The high-resolution XPS N 1s peak (Fig. 1C) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.5, 400.09, and 401.5, which are ascribed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N/amino-N, and graphitic-N, respectively. They revealed that N-GQDs contained predominantly pyrrolic-N and/or amino-N [29,37]. This indicates that N-GQDs possessed surface functional group of nitrogen with a shape of pyrrolic-N and/or amino-N, which plays a key role in creation of the absorption bands in longer wavelength regions, such as first NIR region. Deconvoluting the O1s spectra into two peaks at 531.8 for CO and 533 for CO bonds (Fig. 1C) were also assigned to the epoxide and carbonyl groups on the N-GQDs, which is according to the FTIR analysis [27,38].
Based on the results, FTIR and XPS data could indicate the presence of groups of carbonyl, epoxy, amine, pyridine, and carboxylic acid in N-GQDs.

In the Raman spectrum, two main bands were identified at 1364 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 known as D and G bands, respectively ( Fig. 2A). The D band corresponded to sp2 disordered carbon atoms which linked with sp3 carbon atoms and gave a relative evaluation of the amount of sp3 carbons in the structure. This band included the defects, disorders, functional groups, and foldings, while the G band was ascribed to the sp2 carbons in the graphite-like structure [39,40]. The intensity ratio of these bands (IG/ID) could reflect the amorphous and crystallinity changes in the graphene skeleton. The IG/ID = 1.06 indicated a high level of crystallinity of N-GQDs. Fig. 2B shows a XRD profile of the N-GQDs. The strong diffraction peak of graphite at 23.68° can be assigned to the (002) plane, which confirms the graphene structure of the N-GQDs with high crystallinity and peak at 9.26° can be assigned to the (001) plane of graphene oxide.

Based on the Raman spectrum, it could be inferred that the structure of the N-GQDs consisted of the π-conjugated domains surrounded by amorphous areas with sp3 hybridized carbon. These amorphous areas are responsible for the defects and disorders within the carbon skeleton and are results from self-assembly and conjugation of G0 PAMAM to the N-GQDs.

N-GQDs morphology were evaluated with TEM, HR-TEM and AFM. As seen in the TEM and HR-TEM images, N-GQDs had spherical structures with dimension up to 50 nm ( Fig. 3A). It is also noteworthy that the particles are surrounded by fragments of graphene oxide nanoshells produced during the thermal treatment (Fig. 3C). In addition, some agglomerations are also observed. Fig. 3B represents HR-TEM image of an individual N-GQD. The lattice fringes of the N-GQD with lattice parameter of 0.21 nm, can be clearly observed.

Fig. 4A represents AFM images of N-GQDs and their height profiles. AFM image reveals that the heights of the N-GQDs are about 6 nm and some agglomerations were also observed with a height to 14 nm. It was found that the thickness of each graphene layer ranged from 0.6 to 0.7 nm [41]; thus, they have a different number of layers, which refer to large sizes in the sample. The AFM phase image depicts that N-GQDs were composed of dark and bright dots that could point out to the crystalline and amorphous structures, respectively (Fig. 4B). In the other word, N-GQDs were found to have two identities of crystalline and amorphous; their amorphous structures had a larger size whereas crystalline structures were smaller.
We further measured the surface zeta potential of N-GQDs ( Fig. 5). N-GQDs shows the negative zeta potential having the value of − 9.0 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and photothermal properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–vis spectrum of the sample could be seen in Fig. 6. The spectrum included a broad absorption band which extended from the visible to the NIR with obvious peaks centered at 234, 320 (Fig. 6A), and 560 nm (Fig. 6B) corresponding to the π-π* transition of the aromatic system (sp
2 domain), n-π* transition of sp3 clusters, and surface functional states, respectively [42–44]. A comparison between the N-GQDs and graphene oxide was estimated by calculating the optical band gap (Eg), using the Tauc equation demonstrated in Fig. 7:
αhυ = B (hυ − Eg)γ
where α is the molar extinction coefficient, h is Planck’s constant, ν is incident photon frequency, B is a constant and Eg is the optical band gap and γdonated the nature of the electronic transition responsible for the optical absorption (γ = 1/2 and 2 for direct and indirect band gaps) [45,46]. By extrapolating the linear segment to the intercept of the x-axis, the direct band-gap energy (Eg) are estimated to be 4.6 (Fig. 7A) and 5.45 eV (Fig. 7B) for N-GQDs and graphene oxide respectively.

Fig. 8A illustrates the PL emission of the N-GQDs with the maximum peak at 552 nm upon 480 nm excitation. Under different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 560 nm, the PL emission red-shifted from 503 to 615 (i.e. excitation-dependent emission) (Fig. 8A). The excitation-dependent emission interpreted by the fact that N-GQDs have different emissive origins. N-GQDs showed heterogeneity; in other words, they had various sizes and the surface functional groups as well as aggregation that affected photoluminescence properties similar to absorbance properties. Additionally, they exhibited concentration-dependent PL feature. The inset in Fig. 8A shows the PL emission of the concentrated N-GQDs. The shift in the emission maximum from 552 nm to 615 nm could be easily acquired by increasing the concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solutions. It is believed that high concentrations GQDs in a solution could be result in aggregation. This because of the extremely short distance between individual GQDs and its high surface energy, particularly in the presence of functional groups with opposite charge, which improved the aggregation. Such concentration-induced aggregation causes a red shift at the maximum emission peak and a quenching [47]. As could be seen in Fig. 8B, once the concentration of N-GQDs increase, the obtained samples are seen in black color due to aggregation; meanwhile, the fluorescence emission intensity decrease due to severe quenching. Fig. 8C depicts that the PL excitation spectrum of N-GQDs overlapped with the absorption spectra in the long-wavelength region.
In generally, possible mechanisms of NIR absorption originates in size, surface functional groups, and aggregation of GQDs.

As demonstrated from FTIR and XPS data, the N-GQDs has groups of carbonyl, epoxy, amine, pyridine, and carboxylic acid. This groups could be considered as the possible origin of the dominating band at 560 nm of UV–vis spectrum. It was also found that epoxy and carbonyl groups could result in non-radiative recombination of excitons (localized electron/hole pairs) in graphitic-like structures [39]. Given this fact alongside the presence of the above-mentioned functional groups, it can be suggested that the fractured dendrimers produced during solvothermal reaction could be bonded to N-GQDs and lead to the unusual absorbance (from visible to NIR) of N-GQDs.

As shown in the AFM image, the height of N-GQDs was high, which attributed to the existence of several layers of graphene in the structure of N-GQDs. N-GQDs height is extensively linked to the main structure of GQDs, which could effectively share to their main absorption. The high height size of N-GQDs could result into disruption of planar conjugate structure of sp2 carbon network attributed to the formation of dendrimeric branches surrounding GQDs and affecting photoluminescence and absorption properties [48,49]. The aggregation of N-GQDs and formation of large structures could be another possible mechanism of long wavelengths absorption by N-GQDs. The TEM and AFM images demonstrated that N-GQDs have large sizes up to 50 nm and aggregated. Surface functional groups could be third factor that leads to the extension of the absorption band to NIR. In general, in UV–vis spectrum, the original characteristic peaks of GQDs located at 234 and 320 nm emanated from the main structure of them [32] while the absorption at longer wavelengths might emanate from the surface functional groups of N-GQDs, which have lower intensity than the main characteristics absorptions [29]. Moreover, surface functional groups have opposite charges. Amino, amide, and pyrolic-N groups have positive charges and carboxyl groups have negative charge; this enabled electrostatic interactions between N-GQDs and their agglomeration [28]. Results from zeta potential show that N-GQDs has negative charge, but this charge (− 9.0 mV) is not too high to prevent from N-GQDs aggregation. As a result, their red-shift to the long wavelengths and the increased absorption intensity could be observed. The agglomeration of N-GQDs was verified by photoluminescence spectra in Section 3.1.

Thus, we could propose, dendrimers attach to GQDs through the formation of amide bonds between amino groups of dendrimer and carboxylic acid groups of graphene oxide and then nucleophilic attack of free amino group of dendrimers to the carbonyl group of amide in the same branch is done. This contributed to the formation of the pyrrole rings surrounding of carbonic cores and could explain the origin of the extension in the adsorption band to region NIR and excitation-dependent photoluminescence.

As shown in Fig. 9, approximately 95% of the cells of MCF-7 and 100% of the cells of SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells survived after the incubation with different concentrations of N-GQDs (0–400 μg mL−1) for 24 h, suggesting the excellent biocompatibility of N-GQDs even when the concentration of N-GQDs reached relatively high amounts of 400 μg mL−1.

Given the broad absorption range, N-GQDs was assessed in photothermal therapy of cancer. The temperature changes of N-GQDs aqueous solutions at different concentrations were investigated using continuous irradiation an 808 nm laser (1.29 W/cm2) over a 10 min period ( Fig. 10A). Pure water was taken as the control and its temperature only increased by 5 °C whereas the temperatures of N-GQDs dispersions at concentrations of 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL increased by 21.5, 32, 36.4, and 44.8 °C, respectively (Fig. 10A). The concentration-dependent temperature curves (Fig. 10B) were achieved. The changes in the temperature of N-GQD dispersion with a concentration of 250 μg/mL was also recorded over time until the solution reached the highest temperature, and subsequently, by switching the laser off, the sample cooled down to the ambient temperature (Fig. 10C). Ultimately, the PTT efficiency (η) was determined through the cooling curve results (Fig. 10D) and via Eq. (1) [29].

The calculations and optimization on the structures of the designed graphene oxide and N-GQDs profiles were carried out employing RHF/PM6 method [50]. The quantum mechanics (QM) calculations were performed in the single molecule state. The results of the optimized modeled structure of graphene oxide (A), G0PAMAM (B), and N-GQDs (C) based on the experimental conditions are displayed in Fig. 11. The N-GQDs (C) was obtained through the reaction between the graphene oxide (A) and GOPAMAM (B) under the discussed experimental conditions. In this condition, the N-atoms were doped in structure C). Graphene oxide has been recognized as an electron acceptor associated with the unique optical and electronic characteristics. In 2014, Wen et al. reported an unusual ultrafast electron transfer in the nano composites of “Au” nano-cluster with graphene oxide, in which graphene oxide acts as an electron donor [51–54].

The modeled curved structure of the graphene oxide (A) profile (18.84 Å × 18.15 Å) is depicted in Fig. 11. Based on the QM calculations, the dimensions of the modeled the N-GQDs (C) was 18.76 Å × 18.63 Å.

In Fig. 12(A and B), we could see the HOMO, LUMO energy levels, the energy gap (ΔE
HOMO-LUMO ), and the distribution of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of the optimized modeled structure of graphene oxide (A) and N-GQDs (B).

The comparison of hardness (η) between the graphene oxide and N-doped graphene quantum dot profiles (2.71 and 2.14 eV, respectively) indicated that N-doped graphene quantum dot was more of an electron donor (about Δη; 0.51 eV) than graphene oxide.

As could be seen in Fig. 12, the calculated HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals of the graphene oxide profile were − 8.57 and − 3.16 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the calculated HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals of the N-doped graphene quantum dot profile were − 7.15 and − 2.81 eV, respectively. The HOMO energy level of N-doped graphene quantum dot profile (1.42 eV) was less stable than HOMO of graphene oxide profile. Thus, electron donation property of N-doped graphene quantum dot was better than that of the graphene oxide profile. The LUMO energy level of N-doped graphene quantum dot profile (0.35 eV) was less stable than that of graphene oxide profile. Therefore, electron acceptation property of N-doped graphene quantum dot was better than that of the graphene oxide profile. The energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals could be an index to determine the kinetic stability of the reactants. Accordingly, the kinetic stability of N-doped graphene quantum dot was less than that of graphene oxide. On the other hand, the reactivity of N-doped graphene quantum dot was higher than that of graphene oxide.

Fig. 13 represents the calculated maximum wavelength (in nm) of the optimized modeled structure of graphene oxide (A) and N-doped graphene quantum dot (B). As could be seen, the maximum wavelengths of N-doped graphene quantum dot and graphene oxide were 286 and 230 nm, respectively. Therefore, the doping of N-atoms in the structure of the modeled graphene oxide to produce N-doped graphene quantum dot was about 51 nm red shift (bathochromic shift) in the N-doped graphene quantum dot. the results also revealed the facility of the electron excitation in N-doped graphene quantum dot compared with graphene oxide. The findings of the theoretical aspects of this study, which was performed in the single molecule state, could demonstrate further suitability and ability of N-doped graphene quantum dot to participate in the appropriate processes (reactivity) compared with graphene oxide.

In the current study, N-GQDs with photothermal properties were synthesized via a solvothermal route using G0 PAMAM and graphene oxide as starting materials and H2O2 as the oxidizing agent. Clearly, N-GQDs exhibited a concentration-dependent PL property with a change in the fluorescence emission wavelength from 615 nm to 552 nm. In addition, excitation-dependent PL property observed from N-GQDs that both point out heterogeneous identity of N-GQDs. Moreover, high PTT efficiency (η = 53.45%) was observed in N-GQDs solutions induced with 808-nm laser at 1.29 W/cm2 result from absorption in NIR window. This is in accordance with previous studies. Chemical and morphological analyses together with theoretical optimizations were performed to investigate the structure of N-GQDs and evaluate the origin of the broad absorption band from the visible up to NIR range. We could suggest that the end groups of dendrimers, after conjugation to quantum dots, form pyrrole rings on the surfaces of quantum dots, which may be responsible for the extension in the adsorption band to region NIR and allow a photothermal effect. In addition, this type of conjugation not only strengthens the adsorbent band of carbon dots, but can also be utilized to functionalize quantum dots with drug molecules and other biological reagents. Thus, the current work proposed N-GQDs as promising multifunctional agents for bioimaging, in drug delivery and NIR photothermal therapies.

Sakineh Farajmand-Amirabadi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Scenario design, experimental section and data collection, manuscript preparation and revision. Maryam Maghsudi: Experimental section and revision. Nadereh Rahbar: Revision. Avat(Arman) Taherpour: Computational methods and modeling details. Hadis Alidadi: Experimental section. Azarmidokht Sheini: Funding acquisition. Mohammad Sabaeian: Experimental section. Maryam Kouchak: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank for the financial support of this research work (grant No. N-9811) provided by the Nanotechnology Research Center and Research Council of Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences.
